revision.text:
'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}]]}}
== Overview ==
[[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Danpusan Kofun|ja|断夫山古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, showing the tsukuridashi (center of the image).]] Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|12=draft}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=draft}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=draft}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=draft}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=draft}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=draft}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|12=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|12=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

[[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Platform-style projection]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Platform-style projection]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:Platform-style projection]] [[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:円墳]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Platform-style projection]] [[ja:造出]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]]

'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}]]}}
== Overview ==
[[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Danpusan Kofun|ja|断夫山古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, showing the tsukuridashi (center of the image).]] Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|12=draft}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=draft}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=draft}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=draft}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=draft}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=draft}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|12=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|12=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}]]}}
== Overview ==
[[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Danpusan Kofun|ja|断夫山古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, showing the tsukuridashi (center of the image).]] Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|12=draft}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=draft}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=draft}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=draft}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=draft}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=draft}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|12=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|12=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}]]}}
== Overview ==
[[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Danpusan Kofun|ja|断夫山古墳|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, showing the tsukuridashi (center of the image).]] Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|12=draft}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}]]

Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=draft}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=draft}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=draft}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=draft}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=draft}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|12=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|12=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|12=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
'''造出'''（つくりだし）とは、古墳に直接取り付く、半円形もしくは方形の壇状の施設である。「'''造出し'''」、「'''造り出し'''」とも表記される。中後期の大型[[前方後円墳]]をはじめ、ごく一部の古墳のみに確認されている。 {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|前方後円墳模式図]]}}
== 概要 == [[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|斜め方向から見た[[名古屋市]][[断夫山古墳]]とその造出し（画像中央）]] 造出は前方後円墳、[[前方後方墳]]、[[円墳]]、[[方墳]]のどの墳形でも確認されているが、前方後円墳の例がほとんどである。特に大型の古墳にみられるが、[[宝来山古墳]]のように大王級でも造出のない古墳もあれば、[[新庄二塚古墳]]のように全長60メートル台でも造出のある古墳もあり、付設の有無の基準はよくわかっていない。
造出の性格は古くから論じられ、宮車の車輪模倣説（[[蒲生君平]]）、壺の耳環模倣説（島田貞彦）、殉死者のための埋葬場説（西川宏）などが出されたが、現在では何らかの祭祀の場とする説が広く支持されている<ref>沼澤(2006)p283</ref>。そして、墳丘上で行われていた祭祀が、ある時期から造出で行われるようになったとみられている。
== 成立過程 == [[画像:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|[[赤土山古墳]]の造出し]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|[[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]の造出し]] 大和地域では10期区分の1期～3期の古墳から、墳丘裾周りに方形区画（'''プレ造出'''）を設け、後の造出につながるような祭祀を行っていた例がいくつか知られている<ref name="ex">沼澤(2006)p285</ref>。奈良県天理市の[[中山大塚古墳]]・[[燈籠山古墳]]・[[赤土山古墳]]では後円部背後に壇状施設が確認され、[[東殿塚古墳]]では前方部左（後円部を上にしたときの左）の突出部で祭祀用と思われる土器群が確認されている。また[[行燈山古墳]]・[[渋谷向山古墳]]では後円部右側くびれ部付近に壇状の地形が実測図から確認でき、四世紀半ばごろまでのプレ造出は設置位置が一定していない<ref name="ex" />。
[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]の大王級古墳では、[[佐紀陵山古墳]]の前方部両側面に方形の張り出しが認められ、造出定式化の前段階の施設ととらえられている。続く[[佐紀石塚山古墳]]・[[五社神古墳]]では前方部左側面のくびれ部により近い位置で造出状の地形が確認されており、定式化がさらに進んだ状況を示している<ref>沼澤(2006)p286</ref>。
そして[[古市古墳群]]の[[津堂城山古墳]]をもって、くびれ部付近に造出を付設する一般的な平面形が確立したとみなされている<ref name="ex" />。
== 目的と機能 == 造出は納棺後の祭祀（追善供養）を行うための場であったと考えられている。
[[箸墓古墳]]では後円部から時期の違う二種類の特殊器台が採取されていることから、納棺後ある程度時間がたってから再び祭祀が行われたらしい。その後、たとえ供養のためでも墳丘に登ることをタブー視する考えが広がった結果、追善祭祀の場は墳丘からプレ造出に移行した。そして王墓が王宮のある大和からより遠くの場所に造営されるに従い参加困難になった大王本人に代わって勅使が追善祭祀を執り行うようになり、それに合わせる形で造出の整備・定式化が行われたと考えられている。
納棺儀礼終了後、一定期間の後に追善祭祀が造出で行われ、その期間が終了すると今度は形象埴輪を配置して祭祀の様子を再現し、造出を含めた墳丘への立ち入りはなくなった。最終的にはそれが形骸化し、埴輪による祭祀の再現のみが行われるようになったと考えられている。
また全国で4600基あるとされる前方後円墳のうち、造出のあるものは100基程度（その約半分は奈良・大阪の大型古墳）しか確認されておらず<ref>沼澤(2006)pp296-300</ref>、その数・有無は被葬者の地位を反映しているとされている<ref>ただし前方後円墳よりランクが劣るとされる帆立貝形古墳や前方後方墳、円墳、方墳でも造出のあるものが40基程度確認されており、それはいかなる理由によるものかはよくわかっていない（沼澤 2006）p308</ref>。
== 造出に類似する施設 == === 島状遺構 === 周濠内に独立する方形または円形の遺構。墳丘と接しない点で造出とは異なるが、造出と同じく埴輪群や土器群が出土している。[[津堂城山古墳]]、[[五色塚古墳]]、[[保渡田古墳群|保渡田八幡塚古墳]]、[[保渡田古墳群|井出二子山古墳]]、[[正法寺古墳]]で確認されている。
=== 出島状遺構 === 方形の島状遺構に墳丘と接続する陸橋が付いた遺構で、広義では造出に含まれる。[[三重県]]城之越遺跡や[[奈良県]]南里大東遺跡で確認された導水施設の構造に類似し、また水鳥形埴輪や船形埴輪、井戸形土製品などの水に関係する遺物が多く確認されていることから、水の祭祀が行われていたと考えられている。[[宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|宝塚古墳]]1号墳、[[巣山古墳]]、[[湊茶臼山古墳]]で確認されている。
=== 周堤の突出部 === 周堤もしくは中堤に接続する造出状の施設で、「中堤造出」、「張出」、「別区」、「埴輪祭祀場」などの名称が使われている。造出と同じ機能を持つ施設と思われるが、それより大規模なものが多い。[[埼玉県]]と[[福岡県]]を中心に10基程度確認されている<ref>[[埼玉県]][[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]・[[二子山古墳 (行田市)|二子山古墳]]・[[鉄砲山古墳]]・[[将軍山古墳]]・[[若小玉古墳群|三宝塚古墳]]、[[群馬県]][[七輿山古墳]]、[[長野県]][[正清寺古墳]]、[[大阪府]][[今城塚古墳]]、[[福岡県]][[岩戸山古墳]]・[[東光寺剣塚古墳]]・[[扇八幡古墳]]。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *中井正幸「墳丘に付随する施設」『墳墓構造と葬送祭祀』、同成社、2011年11月 *沼澤豊「前方後円墳と帆立貝古墳」、雄山閣、2006年11月
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の大規模古墳一覧]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つくりたし}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:日本の考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[造出]] to [[Platform-style projection]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

== 造出に類似する施設 == === 島状遺構 === 周濠内に独立する方形または円形の遺構。墳丘と接しない点で造出とは異なるが、造出と同じく埴輪群や土器群が出土している。[[津堂城山古墳]]、[[五色塚古墳]]、[[保渡田古墳群|保渡田八幡塚古墳]]、[[保渡田古墳群|井出二子山古墳]]、[[正法寺古墳]]で確認されている。
=== 出島状遺構 === 方形の島状遺構に墳丘と接続する陸橋が付いた遺構で、広義では造出に含まれる。[[三重県]]城之越遺跡や[[奈良県]]南里大東遺跡で確認された導水施設の構造に類似し、また水鳥形埴輪や船形埴輪、井戸形土製品などの水に関係する遺物が多く確認されていることから、水の祭祀が行われていたと考えられている。[[宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|宝塚古墳]]1号墳、[[巣山古墳]]、[[湊茶臼山古墳]]で確認されている。
=== 周堤の突出部 === 周堤もしくは中堤に接続する造出状の施設で、「中堤造出」、「張出」、「別区」、「埴輪祭祀場」などの名称が使われている。造出と同じ機能を持つ施設と思われるが、それより大規模なものが多い。[[埼玉県]]と[[福岡県]]を中心に10基程度確認されている<ref>[[埼玉県]][[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]・[[二子山古墳 (行田市)|二子山古墳]]・[[鉄砲山古墳]]・[[将軍山古墳]]・[[若小玉古墳群|三宝塚古墳]]、[[群馬県]][[七輿山古墳]]、[[長野県]][[正清寺古墳]]、[[大阪府]][[今城塚古墳]]、[[福岡県]][[岩戸山古墳]]・[[東光寺剣塚古墳]]・[[扇八幡古墳]]。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *中井正幸「墳丘に付随する施設」『墳墓構造と葬送祭祀』、同成社、2011年11月 *沼澤豊「前方後円墳と帆立貝古墳」、雄山閣、2006年11月
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の大規模古墳一覧]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つくりたし}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:日本の考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}

Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|qq=}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatsuchiyama Kofun|qq=}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|qq=}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|前方後方墳|" to "|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|"
revision.text:
'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|qq=}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatsuchiyama Kofun|qq=}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|qq=}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

[[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]]
}}

In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />

The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />

== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Hodota Kofun Group]] → [[Hodota Kofun Cluster]]
revision.text:
'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|qq=}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|ja|赤土山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatsuchiyama Kofun|qq=}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|qq=}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|lt=Hodota Kofun Cluster|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

[[file:CG Danpusan Kofun from above 80m 7p.JPG|250px|thumb|right|{{ill|Danpusan Kofun|ja|断夫山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danpusan Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoya|qq=}}, showing the tsukuridashi (center of the image).]] [[sn:Platform-style projection]] [[ja:造出]]

== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshikizuka Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|lt=Hodota Kofun Cluster|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hodota Kofun Group|qq=}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|ja|宝塚古墳 (松阪市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|qq=}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}
