It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}. {{右|[[File:ZenpokoenfunMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二塚古墳 (葛城市)}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|en|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|WD=Q85480062|lt=Akatsuchiyama Kofun|1=Akatsuchiyama Kofun}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|en|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|WD=Q1107060|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|1=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|en|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|WD=Q85480062|lt=Akatsuchiyama Kofun|1=Akatsuchiyama Kofun}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|en|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|WD=Q1107060|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|1=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Masayuki Nakai, *Facilities Associated with Kofuns,* Dōseisha, 2011. * Toyo Numazawa, *Keyhole-shaped Tumuli and Scallop-shaped Tumuli,* Yūzankaku, 2006. == See Also == * {{ill|List of large kofun in Japan|ja|日本の大規模古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of large kofun in Japan|qq=}}

revision.text:
'''Tsukuridashi''' (造出) refers to semicircular or rectangular platform-like facilities attached directly to ancient Japanese tombs (kofun). It is also written as "Tsukuridashi" or "Tsuridashi" and is observed in only a small number of kofun, primarily mid-to-late period large-scale {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}}. {{右|[[File:Zenpō-kōen-funMoshikizu.svg|thumb|220px|right|Diagram of a {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}}]]}}
== Overview ==
Tsukuridashi has been confirmed in keyhole-shaped tumuli, {{ill|Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular keyhole-shaped mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Round tumuli|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and {{ill|Square tumuli|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, but it is predominantly found in keyhole-shaped tumuli. While large kofun often include tsukuridashi, some royal-level tombs, such as the {{ill|Hōraiyama Kofun|ja|宝来山古墳|1=Hōraiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraiyama Kofun|qq=}}, lack them. Conversely, smaller kofun like the {{ill|Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja|新庄二塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futazuka Kofun (Katsuragi)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二塚古墳 (葛城市)}}, measuring only 60 meters, include them. The criteria for including tsukuridashi remain unclear.
The function of tsukuridashi has been debated historically. Early theories include mimicry of wagon wheels (Gamō Kunpei), imitation of jar handles (Tadahiko Shimada), and burial spaces for sacrificial victims (Hiroshi Nishikawa). The widely accepted view today is that they were ritual spaces.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 283.</ref> It is believed that rituals performed on the Kofun were eventually relocated to the tsukuridashi.
== Development ==
[[File:Akatsuchiyama-kofun-2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|en|Akatsuchiyama Kofun|WD=Q85480062|lt=Akatsuchiyama Kofun|1=Akatsuchiyama Kofun}}]] [[File:Tsukuridashi of Inariyama Tumulus.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Tsukuridashi of the {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}]]
In the Yamato region, early kofun from phases 1 to 3 of a 10-phase classification include square sections near the mound's base, referred to as "pre-tsukuridashi," for performing rituals.<ref name="ex">Numazawa (2006), p. 285.</ref> Examples include platform-like structures behind the rear sections of the {{ill|Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|中山大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōrōyama Kofun|ja|燈籠山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrōyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Akatsuchiyama Kofun in Nara Prefecture. Ritual pottery clusters have been found on protruding sections, such as the left side of the front section of the {{ill|Higashitono Kofun|ja|東殿塚古墳|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitono Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. In some cases, such as the {{ill|Andonyama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andonyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|渋谷向山古墳|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibuya Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}}, platform-like terrain has been identified near the neck of the rear section.<ref name="ex" />
In the royal-scale tombs of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, rectangular protrusions on both sides of the front section of the {{ill|Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|qq=}} are considered a precursor to standardized tsukuridashi. Further advancements are observed in the {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Gosashi Kofun|ja|五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|2=ja|3=五社神古墳|4=fr|5=Tombe de Gosashi|6=zh|7=五社神古墳|1=Gosashi Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosashi Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, where tsukuridashi appear closer to the neck of the front section.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 286.</ref>
The layout was standardized with the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, where tsukuridashi were attached near the neck of the tumuli.<ref name="ex" />
== Purpose and Function ==
Tsukuridashi are thought to have been spaces for post-burial rituals (memorial services). At the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, two types of special pedestals, used for rituals, were recovered from the rear section, indicating rituals took place some time after burial. Over time, climbing the mound for rituals was deemed taboo, shifting ceremonies to the pre-tsukuridashi. As royal tombs were constructed farther from the Yamato region, imperial envoys conducted memorial rituals in place of the emperor. This shift likely prompted the standardization of tsukuridashi.
Following burial rituals, memorial ceremonies were held at tsukuridashi, after which access to the mound ceased, and the tsukuridashi were decorated with figurative {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} to replicate ritual scenes. Eventually, this practice was reduced to symbolic replication using haniwa.<ref>Numazawa (2006), pp. 296–300.</ref>
Out of approximately 4,600 keyhole-shaped tumuli nationwide, only about 100 have tsukuridashi, with about half located in large tombs in Nara and Osaka. Their presence or absence is believed to reflect the status of the interred.<ref>Numazawa (2006), p. 308.</ref>
== Related Facilities ==
=== Island-like Structures === Independent rectangular or circular structures within moats, unconnected to the mound. Examples include the {{ill|Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|ja|津堂城山古墳|1=Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō Shiroyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsudō-Shiroyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}}, and {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}}.
=== Peninsula-like Protrusions === Structures connected to the mound by a land bridge. Examples include the {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|en|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|WD=Q1107060|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|1=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)}} and {{ill|Minato Chausuyama Kofun|ja|湊茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}.
=== Protrusions from Moats === Protrusions extending from the moat or middle embankments. Known as "nakate tsukuridashi," they are larger than typical tsukuridashi. Examples include the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|ja|稲荷山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596864}}, {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|ja|二子山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}.
